events.title: Munich Agreement Signed
events.detail: Britain, France, Italy, and Germany signed the Munich Agreement, allowing Nazi Germany to annex the Sudetenland from Czechoslovakia. British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain returned declaring 'peace for our time,' a promise that would prove catastrophically wrong. The agreement emboldened Hitler and demonstrated that Western democracies would not challenge Nazi expansion.

events.title: Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact Signed
events.detail: Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union signed the Molotov–Ribbentrop Non-Aggression Pact in Moscow. The public treaty pledged non-aggression, but a secret protocol divided Eastern Europe into Nazi and Soviet spheres of influence, setting the stage for the partition of Poland. The pact shocked the world and cleared Hitler's eastern flank for the invasion of Poland.

events.title: Germany Invades Poland — War Begins
events.detail: At 4:45 AM, Germany launched a massive invasion of Poland from the north, south, and west using 1.5 million troops, tanks, and aircraft. The Luftwaffe bombed Polish cities while Panzer divisions executed Blitzkrieg tactics. This act of aggression triggered World War II in Europe.

events.title: Britain and France Declare War on Germany
events.detail: Following Germany's refusal to withdraw from Poland, Britain and France declared war on Germany. Prime Minister Chamberlain announced the declaration in a radio address to the British public. The British Empire and Commonwealth subsequently joined, expanding the war's global reach.

events.title: Soviet Union Invades Poland from the East
events.detail: Sixteen days after Germany's invasion, the Soviet Red Army invaded Poland from the east pursuant to the secret Molotov–Ribbentrop protocol. Facing enemies on two fronts and no effective international aid, Polish forces were overwhelmed. Poland was partitioned between Germany and the USSR by September 28.

sources.name: Sanford, G., 'Katyn and the Soviet Massacre of 1940'

events.title: Soviet Union Attacks Finland — The Winter War
events.detail: The Soviet Union launched a massive invasion of Finland after Finland refused Soviet territorial demands. Despite overwhelming Soviet numbers, Finnish forces inflicted disproportionate losses using guerrilla tactics, ski troops, and defensive fortifications (the Mannerheim Line). The war lasted until March 12, 1940, and exposed serious weaknesses in the Red Army.

events.title: The Phoney War on the Western Front
events.detail: After declaring war on Germany, Britain and France took no major offensive action in the West for eight months — a period called the 'Phoney War' or 'Sitzkrieg.' Both sides manned their defensive positions along the Franco-German border while Germany completed the conquest of Poland. This inaction would later be criticized as a missed opportunity to strike Germany while it was focused eastward.

events.title: Battle of the Atlantic Begins
events.detail: Hours after Britain declared war, German U-boat U-30 sank the British passenger liner SS Athenia, killing 112 civilians. This opened the Battle of the Atlantic, the longest continuous military campaign of WWII, lasting until Germany's defeat in 1945. German submarines would ultimately sink over 3,500 Allied merchant ships.

events.title: Katyn Massacre — Soviet Execution of Polish Officers
events.detail: NKVD agents executed approximately 22,000 Polish military officers, police, intellectuals, and other prisoners in Katyn Forest and other sites by order of Stalin and the Politburo. The massacre decapitated Poland's leadership class and was concealed by the USSR until 1990. In 2010, Russia formally acknowledged Soviet responsibility.

sources.name: Soviet Politburo Order No. 794/B, March 5, 1940 – declassified 1992

events.title: Germany Invades Denmark and Norway
events.detail: Germany launched Operation Weserübung, simultaneously invading Denmark and Norway to secure iron ore routes and strategic Atlantic bases. Denmark capitulated within hours; Norway fought until June 10, aided briefly by British and French forces. The Norwegian campaign ended in German victory but cost the Kriegsmarine significant naval losses.

sources.name: Derry, T.K., 'The Campaign in Norway', HMSO Official History

events.title: Churchill Becomes British Prime Minister
events.detail: Winston Churchill replaced Neville Chamberlain as Prime Minister on the same day Germany launched its Western offensive. Churchill formed a National Government and immediately signaled a policy of total war, famously declaring he had 'nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears and sweat.' His leadership proved vital in sustaining British resistance.

sources.name: Hansard – Churchill's First Speech as PM, May 13, 1940

events.title: Germany Launches Western Offensive — Fall of France
events.detail: Germany invaded France, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg in a sweeping Blitzkrieg attack. German Panzer forces drove through the Ardennes forest, bypassing the Maginot Line and encircling Allied forces in Belgium. France fell in just six weeks — an astonishing collapse of the world's largest army.

sources.name: May, E., 'Strange Victory: Hitler's Conquest of France'

events.title: Operation Dynamo — Dunkirk Evacuation
events.detail: With 400,000 Allied troops encircled at Dunkirk, Britain launched Operation Dynamo, evacuating 338,226 soldiers across the English Channel using Royal Navy vessels and a famous 'armada' of civilian boats. The evacuation saved the core of the British Army but left vast equipment behind. Churchill called it a 'miracle of deliverance.'

sources.name: Royal Navy Historical Branch – Operation Dynamo records
Sebag-Montefiore, H., 'Dunkirk: Fight to the Last Man'

events.title: Italy Declares War on Britain and France
events.detail: Mussolini declared war on Britain and France, calculating that a quick German victory would allow Italy to claim territorial prizes in the Mediterranean and Africa. Italy's entry opened new theaters of war in North Africa and the Mediterranean. Italian forces were poorly equipped and trained compared to German standards.

sources.name: Italian State Archives – Archivio Centrale dello Stato

events.title: France Signs Armistice — Vichy Government Formed
events.detail: France signed an armistice with Germany in the same railway car where Germany accepted defeat in WWI. Northern France and the Atlantic coast came under German occupation; southern France became the collaborationist 'Vichy' regime under Marshal Pétain. General de Gaulle fled to London and continued resistance from exile.

sources.name: Paxton, R., 'Vichy France: Old Guard and New Order'

events.title: Battle of Britain — RAF Defeats Luftwaffe
events.detail: Germany launched an air campaign to destroy the RAF as a precondition for the planned invasion of Britain (Operation Sea Lion). RAF Fighter Command, aided by radar and Ultra intelligence, defeated the Luftwaffe in three months of intense aerial combat. By October 1940, Hitler postponed the invasion indefinitely — Britain's first major Allied victory.

events.title: The Blitz — Germany Bombs British Cities
events.detail: After failing to destroy the RAF, Germany shifted to bombing British cities — the Blitz — targeting London nightly for 57 consecutive nights. Over the following months, the Luftwaffe bombed Coventry, Birmingham, Liverpool, Plymouth, and other industrial centers. Approximately 43,000 British civilians died in the Blitz, but morale held.

events.title: Tripartite Pact — Axis Alliance Formalized
events.detail: Germany, Italy, and Japan signed the Tripartite Pact in Berlin, formalizing the Axis alliance. The pact created a mutual defense obligation and divided spheres of influence: Europe under Germany and Italy, Asia under Japan. Spain, Hungary, Romania, and Slovakia later adhered to the pact.

sources.name: German Foreign Ministry Archives – Tripartite Pact text

events.title: North Africa Campaign Opens
events.detail: Italian forces under Marshal Graziani invaded Egypt from Libya, beginning the North Africa campaign. The British Eighth Army under General Wavell mounted a counter-offensive (Operation Compass) that devastated Italian forces in December 1940. Germany would deploy the Afrika Korps under Rommel in early 1941 to rescue the Italian position.

sources.name: UK National Archives – WO 201 Middle East Command records
Playfair, I.S.O., 'The Mediterranean and Middle East', HMSO Official History

events.title: Lend-Lease Act — US Supplies Allies
events.detail: President Roosevelt signed the Lend-Lease Act, authorizing the US to supply Britain and later the USSR and other Allies with war materials without requiring immediate payment. Lend-Lease ultimately transferred $50 billion in goods (equivalent to ~$650 billion today) to Allied nations. It marked a decisive end to US neutrality short of direct entry into the war.

events.title: Operation Barbarossa — Germany Invades the Soviet Union
events.detail: Germany launched the largest land invasion in history, deploying 3.8 million Axis troops along a 2,900-km front against the Soviet Union. The surprise attack initially routed the Red Army, capturing millions of prisoners and vast territory. However, Hitler's decision to divide forces among three army groups — Leningrad, Moscow, and Kyiv — dispersed strength and slowed the advance before Moscow.

sources.name: Glantz, D., 'Barbarossa: Hitler's Invasion of Russia 1941'

events.title: Siege of Leningrad Begins
events.detail: German and Finnish forces encircled Leningrad (St. Petersburg), beginning a 872-day siege — the longest in modern warfare. Over 800,000 civilians died, mostly from starvation during the brutal blockade winters. The city held out through extraordinary civilian endurance until relief in January 1944.

sources.name: Russian State Archive of Literature and Art – Leningrad diaries
Salisbury, H., 'The 900 Days: The Siege of Leningrad'

events.title: Atlantic Charter — Roosevelt and Churchill Define War Aims
events.detail: Churchill and Roosevelt met aboard warships off Newfoundland and issued the Atlantic Charter, outlining Allied war aims: no territorial aggrandizement, self-determination of peoples, freedom from fear and want, and freedom of the seas. The Charter became the foundation for the United Nations and post-war international order.

sources.name: US Department of State – Atlantic Charter text

events.title: Japan Attacks Pearl Harbor — US Enters the War
events.detail: Japan launched a surprise carrier-based air attack on the US Pacific Fleet at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, sinking or damaging 8 battleships, killing 2,403 Americans, and wounding 1,178 more. The attack was coordinated with simultaneous strikes across Southeast Asia. President Roosevelt called it 'a date which will live in infamy' and Congress declared war on Japan the following day.

sources.name: NARA – Pearl Harbor Attack Congressional Hearings, 1945–1946

events.title: Germany and Italy Declare War on the United States
events.detail: Four days after Pearl Harbor, Hitler declared war on the United States, honoring his Axis pact with Japan and relieving Roosevelt of a political dilemma about entering the European war. This decision transformed the conflict into a truly global war and ultimately sealed Germany's fate by bringing America's industrial and military power to bear against it.

events.title: Fall of Singapore — Britain's Greatest Military Defeat
events.detail: Japanese forces under General Yamashita captured Singapore after a 70-day campaign down the Malay Peninsula, forcing 85,000 British, Australian, and Indian troops to surrender — Britain's greatest military disaster. Churchill called it 'the worst disaster and largest capitulation in British history.' Singapore's capture gave Japan control of Southeast Asia.

sources.name: UK National Archives WO 172 – Malaya Command records

events.title: Wannsee Conference — Final Solution Coordinated
events.detail: Fifteen senior Nazi officials met at a lakeside villa in Wannsee, Berlin to coordinate the 'Final Solution to the Jewish Question' — the systematic genocide of all European Jews. Chaired by SS-Obergruppenführer Reinhard Heydrich, the conference planned the murder of 11 million Jews across Europe. The Wannsee Protocol is one of WWII's most damning primary source documents.

sources.name: Wannsee Protocol – Nuremberg Document NG-2586 (Nuremberg Trials)

events.title: US Executive Order 9066 — Japanese-American Internment
events.detail: President Roosevelt signed Executive Order 9066, authorizing the forced removal of 120,000 Japanese-Americans from the West Coast to inland internment camps. Most were American citizens; none were ever found guilty of espionage or sabotage. In 1988, the US government formally apologized and paid reparations under the Civil Liberties Act.

sources.name: Commission on Wartime Relocation, 'Personal Justice Denied', 1983

events.title: Allied Decision to Invade North Africa (Operation Torch)
events.detail: Roosevelt, Churchill, and their commanders agreed to launch a second front in North Africa rather than France in 1942, over strong Soviet objections. Operation Torch would land in Morocco and Algeria in November 1942, aiming to squeeze Axis forces between the US Eighth Army from the west and British forces from the east. The decision shaped Allied Mediterranean strategy for 1943.

sources.name: Combined Chiefs of Staff records – NARA RG 218

events.title: Battle of Midway — Pacific War's Turning Point
events.detail: US carrier aircraft, guided by codebreakers who had cracked Japanese naval codes, sank four Japanese fleet carriers at Midway Atoll in a stunning 5-minute dive-bomber attack. The four carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Sōryū, and Hiryū — represented the core of Japan's striking power. Midway shifted naval supremacy in the Pacific to the United States.

events.title: Second Battle of El Alamein — Rommel Defeated
events.detail: General Montgomery's British Eighth Army launched a massive 13-day offensive against Rommel's Afrika Korps at El Alamein, Egypt, breaking through Axis lines and beginning the final expulsion of Axis forces from North Africa. Churchill called it 'the end of the beginning.' Combined with Operation Torch, Axis forces in Africa were trapped in Tunisia.

sources.name: UK National Archives WO 169 – 8th Army records
Barr, N., 'Pendulum of War: The Three Battles of El Alamein'

events.title: Operation Torch — Allied Landings in North Africa
events.detail: US and British forces under General Eisenhower landed in Morocco and Algeria, opening a second front against Axis forces in Africa. Initially encountering Vichy French resistance, the Allies quickly advanced eastward toward Tunisia. This was America's first major offensive operation in the European theater and a crucial test of Allied coordination.

events.title: Battle of Stalingrad — War's Bloodiest Battle Begins
events.detail: Germany's 6th Army under General Paulus launched an all-out assault on Stalingrad on the Volga River, where fighting devolved into brutal street combat for every building. Soviet forces under General Chuikov defended the city in savage close-quarters battles. The battle lasted six months and became the bloodiest single engagement in human history.

events.title: Operation Uranus — Soviet Counteroffensive Encircles German 6th Army
events.detail: Soviet forces launched Operation Uranus, a massive pincer attack north and south of Stalingrad that encircled the German 6th Army (330,000 men) within days. Hitler refused to allow Paulus to break out. A relief operation (Operation Winter Storm) failed. The encircled army slowly starved and froze through the winter of 1942–43.

sources.name: Russian State Military Archive RGVA – Operation Uranus orders

events.title: Paulus Surrenders at Stalingrad — Germany's Greatest Defeat
events.detail: Field Marshal Friedrich Paulus — just promoted by Hitler, who expected him to die rather than surrender — capitulated at Stalingrad with 91,000 surviving troops. Only 6,000 would return to Germany after the war. Stalingrad marked the definitive strategic turning point on the Eastern Front and shattered the myth of German invincibility.

events.title: Warsaw Ghetto Uprising — Largest Armed Jewish Revolt of the Holocaust
events.detail: On the eve of Passover, the Jewish Combat Organization (ŻOB) under 24-year-old Mordechai Anielewicz launched an armed revolt against SS forces arriving to liquidate Warsaw's remaining 60,000 ghetto inhabitants. Approximately 700 fighters armed with smuggled pistols, rifles, and improvised weapons held off 2,000+ SS troops and Wehrmacht soldiers for 28 days. SS General Jürgen Stroop systematically burned the ghetto block by block to suppress the uprising. Anielewicz and his command staff died at the ŻOB bunker at 18 Miła Street on May 8. The uprising ended on May 16, 1943, when Stroop detonated the Great Synagogue of Warsaw as a symbol of Nazi victory. The revolt is the largest single act of Jewish armed resistance during the Holocaust and is commemorated annually on April 19.

events.title: Axis Forces Surrender in Tunisia — North Africa Campaign Ends
events.detail: Over 230,000 Axis troops surrendered in Tunisia, ending the North Africa campaign. The scale of the surrender rivaled Stalingrad. Allied victory in Africa opened the way for the invasion of Sicily and Italy. The campaign had cost the Axis two years of strategic time and irreplaceable forces.

events.title: Battle of Kursk — Largest Tank Battle in History
events.detail: Germany launched Operation Citadel to eliminate the Kursk salient with 780,000 troops and 2,900 tanks. Soviet intelligence had forewarned of the attack, allowing massive defensive preparations. The battle resulted in Germany's last major offensive on the Eastern Front, after which the strategic initiative permanently shifted to the Soviets.

sources.name: German Federal Archives BA-MA – OKH Kursk records
Glantz, D. & House, J., 'The Battle of Kursk'

events.title: Operation Husky — Allied Invasion of Sicily
events.detail: American and British forces landed on Sicily in the largest amphibious operation in history to that point (160,000 troops on the first day). Despite fierce resistance from German forces, Sicily fell within 38 days. The campaign directly triggered the fall of Mussolini's government and Italy's armistice negotiations.

sources.name: D'Este, C., 'Bitter Victory: The Battle for Sicily'

events.title: Mussolini Arrested — Italian Fascism Collapses
events.detail: The Italian Fascist Grand Council voted to remove Mussolini from power; King Victor Emmanuel III had him arrested. Marshal Badoglio formed a new government and began secret armistice negotiations with the Allies. Germany responded by occupying Italy, rescuing Mussolini in a daring glider raid (September 12), and installing him in a puppet state (Italian Social Republic).

sources.name: Italian State Archives – Verbale del Gran Consiglio, July 25, 1943

events.title: Italy Surrenders — Armistice with Allies
events.detail: Italy signed an armistice with the Allies, announced simultaneously with Allied landings at Salerno. Germany immediately disarmed Italian forces, occupied northern and central Italy, and deported thousands of Italian Jews to death camps. The Italian campaign would continue as a brutal slog up the peninsula until May 1945.

sources.name: Armistice of Cassibile – Eisenhower's Headquarters records NARA

events.title: Guadalcanal Campaign — First US Ground Offensive in Pacific
events.detail: US Marines landed on Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands, beginning a six-month campaign to seize the island and its vital airstrip. The campaign involved intense ground fighting in jungle conditions, major naval battles, and sustained aerial combat. Japan's defeat at Guadalcanal in February 1943 marked the first significant US ground victory in the Pacific.

sources.name: Frank, R., 'Guadalcanal: The Definitive Account of the Landmark Battle'

events.title: D-Day — Allied Invasion of Normandy
events.detail: Operation Overlord launched the largest amphibious invasion in history: 156,000 Allied troops landed on five Normandy beaches under massive naval and air support. American forces suffered heavy losses at Omaha Beach; other beaches were secured more quickly. The Normandy landings opened the long-awaited second front in Western Europe and began the liberation of France.

sources.name: NARA – SHAEF records RG 331; 21st Army Group records

events.title: Operation Market Garden — Airborne Assault in Netherlands
events.detail: Montgomery's ambitious plan to end the war by Christmas 1944 used 35,000 paratroopers to capture bridges across the Dutch rivers, with ground forces linking them up. At Arnhem, the British 1st Airborne Division landed too far from its bridge and was surrounded; only 2,400 of 10,000 escaped. 'A bridge too far' became a byword for overreach.

sources.name: UK National Archives WO 205 – 21st Army Group records

events.title: Liberation of Paris
events.detail: Free French forces of General Leclerc's 2nd Armored Division entered Paris, followed by General de Gaulle who marched down the Champs-Élysées amidst massive celebrations. German commander General von Choltitz had defied Hitler's order to destroy Paris. De Gaulle declared the liberation a great French achievement, downplaying the vital American role.

sources.name: French National Archives – Provisional Government records
Collins, L. & Lapierre, D., 'Is Paris Burning?'

events.title: Battle of the Bulge — Germany's Last Major Offensive in West
events.detail: Germany launched its last major offensive in the West through the Ardennes Forest, achieving complete surprise and driving a 'bulge' 85 km deep into Allied lines. Surrounded at Bastogne, General McAuliffe famously replied 'Nuts!' to German surrender demands. The offensive ultimately failed, exhausting Germany's last armored reserves.

sources.name: MacDonald, C., 'A Time for Trumpets: The Battle of the Bulge'

events.title: Warsaw Uprising — Polish Home Army Rises Against Nazis
events.detail: The Polish Home Army (Armia Krajowa) launched a major uprising against German occupation in Warsaw, expecting Soviet forces approaching from the east to assist. Stalin halted Soviet advances, denying air access to Western Allied supply missions for weeks. Germany crushed the uprising after 63 days; Warsaw was systematically destroyed and 200,000 civilians killed.

sources.name: Davies, N., 'Rising '44: The Battle for Warsaw'

events.title: Operation Bagration — Soviet Summer Offensive Destroys Army Group Centre
events.detail: The Soviet Red Army launched Operation Bagration against German Army Group Centre, the largest Allied offensive of WWII. Using elaborate deception operations, Soviet forces shattered 28 German divisions, killing or capturing 300,000 troops — losses exceeding Stalingrad. The offensive advanced over 600 km in two months and liberated Belorussia.

sources.name: Russian State Military Archive RGVA – Belorussian Front records

events.title: Operation Dragoon — Allied Landings in Southern France
events.detail: US and Free French forces landed on the French Riviera in Operation Dragoon, quickly advancing up the Rhône Valley. The landings liberated southern France within weeks and linked with forces advancing from Normandy. Marseille and Toulon were captured within a fortnight, providing crucial port capacity for Allied logistics.

sources.name: Clarke, J. & Smith, R., 'Riviera to the Rhine', US Army Official History

events.title: Battle of Philippine Sea — 'Great Marianas Turkey Shoot'
events.detail: The largest carrier-versus-carrier battle in history saw the US Navy destroy Japanese naval air power in what became known as 'the Great Marianas Turkey Shoot.' Japan lost 600 aircraft and three carriers; US losses were minimal. The battle ended Japan's ability to mount effective carrier operations for the rest of the war.

sources.name: Morison, S.E., 'History of US Naval Operations in WWII', Vol. VIII

events.title: Bretton Woods Conference — Post-War Economic Order
events.detail: 44 Allied nations met at Bretton Woods, New Hampshire to design the post-war international monetary system. The conference created the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank, and established the US dollar as the global reserve currency pegged to gold. The Bretton Woods system shaped global finance until 1971.

sources.name: US Treasury Department – Bretton Woods Conference proceedings

events.title: Soviet Forces Liberate Auschwitz-Birkenau
events.detail: Soldiers of the Soviet 60th Army liberated the Auschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp complex, finding 7,000 surviving prisoners amid evidence of mass murder on an industrial scale. Over 1.1 million people, predominantly Jews, had been murdered at Auschwitz. January 27 is now designated International Holocaust Remembrance Day by the United Nations.

events.title: Yalta Conference — Big Three Shape Post-War World
events.detail: Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin met at Yalta in Crimea to plan the final defeat of Germany and design the post-war international order. Stalin secured control over Eastern Europe and a Soviet role in the Pacific war in exchange for agreeing to the United Nations. The agreements made at Yalta were contested as a Western betrayal of Eastern European nations.

sources.name: US Department of State – Foreign Relations of the United States, Yalta Conference 1945

events.title: Battle of Iwo Jima
events.detail: US Marines assaulted the heavily fortified island of Iwo Jima, defended by 21,000 Japanese troops in a labyrinth of tunnels and bunkers. The 36-day battle cost nearly 7,000 Marine dead and 19,000 wounded. The iconic flag-raising on Mount Suribachi became one of WWII's most famous images. Almost all Japanese defenders died.

events.title: Firebombing of Dresden
events.detail: British and American bombers conducted four raids on the German city of Dresden, creating massive firestorms that destroyed the city center. Post-war estimates of deaths ranged from 22,700 to 25,000 (confirmed by a 2010 German commission) though wartime propaganda inflated figures to 200,000+. The bombing's military necessity and proportionality remains debated by historians.

events.title: Battle of Berlin — Final Assault on the German Capital
events.detail: Soviet Marshal Zhukov's forces launched a massive assault on Berlin with 2.5 million troops and 6,250 tanks. Intense house-to-house fighting raged through the ruined capital for two weeks. An estimated 100,000 German civilians died during the battle, along with 80,000 Soviet soldiers and the entire garrison of Berlin.

sources.name: Russian State Military Archive RGVA – 1st Belorussian Front records

events.title: Hitler Dies by Suicide in Berlin Bunker
events.detail: With Soviet forces fighting street-to-street battles less than a kilometer away, Adolf Hitler shot himself in the head in his underground bunker (Führerbunker) in Berlin; Eva Braun, whom he had married hours earlier, took cyanide. Their bodies were burned in the Reich Chancellery garden per Hitler's instructions. Grand Admiral Dönitz was named Hitler's successor.

sources.name: Soviet NKVD interrogation records of Hitler's staff, 1945 (declassified)

events.title: Germany Surrenders — V-E Day
events.detail: Germany signed the unconditional surrender instrument at Reims (May 7) and Berlin-Karlshorst (May 8), accepted by Allied Supreme Commander Eisenhower and Soviet Marshal Zhukov. V-E Day (Victory in Europe) was celebrated across the Allied world. The war in Europe had lasted 2,174 days from the invasion of Poland.

sources.name: German Act of Military Surrender, May 8, 1945 – NARA
Imperial War Museum – V-E Day Collection

events.title: Potsdam Conference — Final Allied Summit
events.detail: Truman, Churchill (then Attlee), and Stalin met near Berlin at Potsdam to settle German occupation zones, issue an ultimatum to Japan (the Potsdam Declaration), and plan the post-war order. Truman secretly informed Stalin that the US had 'a new weapon of unusual destructive force' — the atomic bomb, tested the day before. The conference finalized the division of Germany and Europe.

sources.name: US Department of State – Foreign Relations of the United States, Potsdam 1945
Gaddis, J.L., 'The Cold War: A New History'

events.title: Atomic Bomb Dropped on Hiroshima
events.detail: The B-29 Enola Gay dropped 'Little Boy,' a uranium gun-type atomic bomb, on Hiroshima, destroying 4.7 square miles of the city and killing an estimated 70,000–80,000 people instantly. Total deaths by year's end reached 90,000–140,000, including radiation sickness victims. It was the first use of a nuclear weapon in warfare.

sources.name: US Strategic Bombing Survey, Pacific War – NARA

events.title: Soviet Union Declares War on Japan — Manchuria Invaded
events.detail: The USSR declared war on Japan and launched Operation August Storm, a massive invasion of Japanese-held Manchuria with 1.5 million troops. Within days, Soviet forces shattered the Kwantung Army, Japan's largest overseas force. This Soviet entry, combined with the atomic bombs, convinced Japan's Supreme War Council that continuing the war was hopeless.

sources.name: Russian State Military Archive – August Storm operation records
Glantz, D., 'The Soviet Strategic Offensive in Manchuria, 1945'

events.title: Atomic Bomb Dropped on Nagasaki
events.detail: The B-29 Bockscar dropped 'Fat Man,' a plutonium implosion bomb, on Nagasaki, killing 40,000–50,000 immediately and 60,000–80,000 total by year's end. The bomb detonated 2 miles off target due to cloud cover, limiting but not preventing catastrophic destruction. Combined with Soviet entry into the war, these events forced Japan's surrender.

sources.name: US Strategic Bombing Survey, Pacific War – NARA

events.title: Japan Surrenders — V-J Day
events.detail: Emperor Hirohito broadcast his surrender announcement to the Japanese people — the first time most had heard his voice. The formal surrender was signed aboard USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay on September 2. The Pacific War had lasted 1,347 days from Pearl Harbor and cost approximately 36 million lives across the Asia-Pacific region.

sources.name: Japanese Imperial Rescript of Surrender, August 15, 1945
National Archives of Japan – Imperial Household Agency records

events.title: Nuremberg Trials Begin — Nazi Leaders on Trial
events.detail: The International Military Tribunal convened at Nuremberg to try 24 major Nazi war criminals for crimes against peace, war crimes, and crimes against humanity. Twelve were sentenced to death, including Göring and Ribbentrop; seven received prison sentences. The trials established the Nuremberg Principles and the modern concept of international criminal law.

events.title: Battle of Okinawa — Bloodiest Pacific Battle
events.detail: The last major battle of the Pacific War, Okinawa involved 287,000 US troops assaulting an island defended by 100,000 Japanese. The 82-day campaign produced catastrophic casualties: 12,000 US dead, 70,000+ wounded, 100,000+ Japanese military dead, and an estimated 100,000–150,000 Okinawan civilian dead. Kamikaze attacks sank 36 US ships.

events.title: President Roosevelt Dies — Truman Assumes Presidency
events.detail: President Franklin D. Roosevelt died of a cerebral hemorrhage at Warm Springs, Georgia, just three weeks before Germany's surrender. He had served as president through the entire US involvement in WWII. Vice President Harry S. Truman was sworn in and would make the fateful decision to use atomic bombs against Japan.

sources.name: National Archives – FDR Presidential Library, Hyde Park

events.title: United Nations Charter Signed in San Francisco
events.detail: Representatives of 50 nations signed the UN Charter in San Francisco, creating the United Nations as the primary international organization for maintaining peace and security. The Charter created the Security Council with five permanent members (US, UK, USSR, France, China) holding veto power. The UN formally came into existence on October 24, 1945.

sources.name: UN Charter – Authenticated original text, UN Archives
